court: European Court of Human Rights (Second Section)

facts.text: The applicants were employed by E.E.T.I., a private enterprise.

anchor.surface_text: 3.  The applicants were employed by E.E.T.I. (hereafter “the company”), a private enterprise. On various dates between 2001 and 2003, following the termination of their employment contracts, some of the applicants in application no. 26878/07 brought judicial proceedings against the company to claim unpaid salary and other related payments.

facts.text: On 19 February, 25 February and 23 August 2003 the İzmir Labour Court delivered judgments against the company, awarding salary arrears and severance pay to some applicants in application no. 26878/07.

anchor.surface_text: On 19 February, 25 February and 23 August 2003 respectively the İzmir Labour Court delivered judgments against the company, awarding those applicants salary arrears and severance pay.

facts.text: Other applicants started execution proceedings against the company via the bailiff’s office to enforce payment of salary arrears.

anchor.surface_text: 4.  Other applicants directly started execution proceedings (icra takibi) against the company via the bailiff’s office and served a confiscation order in order to enforce payment of their salary arrears and other related claims.

facts.text: In 2003 the labour court ruled in favour of applicants who started execution proceedings, annulling the company’s objection and granting 40% compensation.

anchor.surface_text: As a result of the company’s objection to the execution proceedings, the applicants brought cases before the labour court and requested that the court annul the company’s objection and rule on additional compensation of 40% of the debt as a penalty for the company’s objecting to the execution proceedings (icra inkar tazminatı). On various dates in 2003 the labour court ruled in favour of the applicants and granted their claims, including compensation amounting to 40% of the debt.

facts.text: On 26 February 2004 the Commercial Court of İzmir declared the company insolvent.

anchor.surface_text: 5.  On 26 February 2004 the Commercial Court of İzmir declared the company insolvent and ordered the start of the insolvency proceedings.

facts.text: Court of Cassation quashed the insolvency judgment, decision final on 17 March 2005.

anchor.surface_text: On appeal, the Court of Cassation quashed the Commercial Court’s judgment, the cassation decision becoming final on 17 March 2005.

facts.text: On 6 May 2005 the first-instance court declared the company insolvent, final on 7 June 2005.

anchor.surface_text: In the fresh proceedings, the first-instance court declared the company insolvent on 6 May 2005, a decision which became final on 7 June 2005.

facts.text: Liquidation administration ranked unsecured creditors, placing claims of four applicants in 26878/07 and applicant in 32446/07 in fourth rank.

anchor.surface_text: 6.  Following the opening of insolvency proceedings by the decision of 6 May 2005, the liquidation administration established a ranking of unsecured creditors, including the applicants’ work-related claims against the company. That list placed the claims concerning the four applicants in application no. 26878/07 and the applicant in application no. 32446/07, in the fourth and lowest rank,

facts.text: Provisional list of creditors served on applicants on 11 October 2005.

anchor.surface_text: The provisional list was served on the applicants on 11 October 2005.

facts.text: On 14 and 18 October 2005 applicants brought case before Enforcement Court of İzmir challenging ranking.

anchor.surface_text: 7.  On 14 and 18 October 2005, respectively, the applicants brought a case before the Enforcement Court of İzmir. They challenged the ranking assigned to them by the liquidation administration and requested that the court assign priority ranking to all of their work-related claims.

facts.text: Applicants argued provisions of Enforcement and Bankruptcy Act unconstitutional and discriminatory.

anchor.surface_text: During the proceedings, the applicants further argued that the relevant provisions of the Enforcement and Bankruptcy Act, which provided for the priority ranking of labour-related claims only dating back a year from the date of the opening of insolvency proceedings, were unconstitutional for being discriminatory

facts.text: Applicants in 26878/07 requested referral to Constitutional Court.

anchor.surface_text: Moreover, the applicants in application no. 26878/07 requested the domestic court to refer their plea of unconstitutionality to the Constitutional Court.

facts.text: On 29 March and 28 April 2006 Enforcement Court dismissed claims, holding outside one-year limit.

anchor.surface_text: 8.  On 29 March and 28 April 2006, respectively, the court dismissed the applicants’ claims, holding that their claims fell outside the one‑year time‑limit and that therefore they could not be granted priority.

facts.text: Court dismissed request for referral to Constitutional Court.

anchor.surface_text: The court also dismissed the applicants’ request to refer the question of the compatibility of the relevant legislation with the Constitution, holding that the relevant sections of the Enforcement and Bankruptcy Act did not raise an issue of the different treatment of claims.

facts.text: On 14 December 2006 and 1 February 2007 Court of Cassation upheld the judgments.

anchor.surface_text: 9.  On 14 December 2006 and 1 February 2007, respectively, the Court of Cassation upheld the judgments of 29 March and 28 April 2006.

facts.text: Liquidation administration prepared final dividend sheet on unspecified date.

anchor.surface_text: 10.  On an unspecified date, the liquidation administration prepared a final dividend sheet (pay cetveli), the details of which were not made available to the Court.

facts.text: Applicants received certificates for unsatisfied claims; most claims remain unpaid except five applicants.

anchor.surface_text: 11.  On an unspecified date, the applicants were given certificates by the liquidation administration for the amount of their claims that had remained unsatisfied after the distribution of assets. Accordingly, apart from five applicants whose claims were mostly satisfied, the claims of the other applicants remain unpaid (see annexed table).

facts.text: On 30 December 2016 the company was wound up by Commercial Court of İzmir.

anchor.surface_text: 12.  On 30 December 2016 the company was wound up pursuant to the decision of the Commercial Court of İzmir.

facts.text: Applications lodged on 26 June 2007 and 25 July 2007.

anchor.surface_text: Having regard to the above applications lodged on 26 June 2007 and 25 July 2007 respectively,

facts.text: Court sat on 12 December 2017.

anchor.surface_text: The European Court of Human Rights (Second Section), sitting on 12 December 2017 as a Chamber composed of:

facts.text: Section 206 of Enforcement and Bankruptcy Act ranks work-related claims accrued within one year prior to insolvency as priority.

anchor.surface_text: 16.  Law no. 2004 also ranks claims within the class of priority claims. Accordingly, the work-related claims of employees, including severance and notice pay accrued within the year prior to the opening of insolvency proceedings, as well as notice and severance pay accrued because of the termination of an employment contract as a result of the insolvency, are ranked first.

facts.text: Since October 2004 there is a wage guarantee fund covering up to three months unpaid wages in insolvency.

anchor.surface_text: 19.  Since October 2004 former Article 33 of the Labour Code (Law no. 4857) and the provisional section 1 of the Unemployment Insurance Act provide for a wage guarantee fund to protect employees’ entitlements in case of an employer’s insolvency or bankruptcy. The fund covers up to three months of unpaid wages

precedents.citation: Ruiz Torija v. Spain, 9 December 1994, § 29, Series A no. 303-A

anchor.surface_text: Torija v. Spain, 9 December 1994, § 29, Series A no. 303‑A). Furthermore, the Court should not act as a fourth-instance body and will therefore not question under Article 6 § 1 the national courts’ assessment, unless their findings can be regarded as arbitrary or manifestly unreasonable (for a recapitulation of the relevant principles see, as a recent example, Moreira Ferreira v. Portugal (no. 2) [GC], no. 19867/12, §§ 83 and 84, ECHR 2017 (extracts)).

precedents.cited_proposition: courts obliged to give reasons for judgments, but not detailed answer to every argument; extent varies according to nature of decision

precedents.citation: Moreira Ferreira v. Portugal (no. 2) [GC], no. 19867/12, §§ 83 and 84, ECHR 2017 (extracts)

anchor.surface_text: Ferreira v. Portugal (no. 2) [GC], no. 19867/12, §§ 83 and 84, ECHR 2017 (extracts)). 43. The Court further reiterates that the Convention does not guarantee, as such, a right of access to a court with competence to invalidate or override a law, or to give an official interpretation of a law. Neither does it guarantee any right to have a case referred by a domestic court to another national or international authority for a preliminary ruling

precedents.cited_proposition: recapitulation of relevant principles on duty to give reasons under Article 6 § 1; Court not fourth-instance unless arbitrary

anchor.surface_text: Pronina v. Ukraine, no. 63566/00, § 24, 18 July 2006 and the cases cited therein). 44. In the Turkish legal context, where a question of constitutionality arises during judicial proceedings, the courts have discretion in deciding on referring the matter to the Constitutional Court. If the court hearing the case does not regard the issue as raising serious doubts about its compatibility with the Constitution, it may dismiss it by giving reasons (see paragraph 20 above)

precedents.cited_proposition: Convention does not guarantee right to have case referred to another authority for preliminary ruling on constitutionality
